name: Iron Laser Cleaning Dataset
description: Comprehensive laser cleaning parameters and material properties for Iron. Includes thermal, optical, mechanical, and laser interaction properties validated against industry standards.

license.name: Creative Commons Attribution 4.0 International
license.description: Free to share and adapt with attribution

measurementTechnique: Laser ablation testing, material characterization, spectroscopy

includedInDataCatalog.name: Z-Beam Material Properties Database
includedInDataCatalog.description: Comprehensive laser cleaning parameters and material properties for industrial applications

powerRange.description: Optimal average power for Iron oxide removal without substrate damage

wavelength.description: Near-IR wavelength with good absorption in Iron oxides and minimal substrate damage

spotSize.description: Beam spot diameter for efficient cleaning resolution

repetitionRate.description: Optimal repetition rate for continuous cleaning coverage

energyDensity.description: Fluence threshold for effective oxide removal from Iron surface

pulseWidth.description: Nanosecond pulse duration for efficient oxide layer removal

scanSpeed.description: Optimal scanning speed for uniform cleaning coverage

passCount.description: Number of cleaning passes for complete oxide removal

overlapRatio.description: Optimal beam overlap for uniform cleaning without excessive heat accumulation

power.description: Typical range for industrial Nd:YAG laser cleaning of metals like iron; starting at 200W balances efficiency and safety to avoid melting (iron melting point ~1538°C). Max limited to prevent substrate damage via thermal conduction. Source: IPG Photonics YLR series specs and review in Optics and Lasers in Engineering (2018).

scan_speed.description: Effective range for contamination removal on iron; higher speeds (e.g., 2000 mm/s) for light rust/oil, lower (500-1000 mm/s) for heavy oxides to ensure ablation without overheating. Recommended optimizes throughput while minimizing heat-affected zone (HAZ). Source: Industry standards from CleanLaser manuals and study in Journal of Cleaner Production (2020).

pulse_width.description: Nanosecond regime preferred for iron cleaning to provide sufficient energy for contaminant ablation with minimal thermal diffusion (due to iron's high conductivity ~80 W/m·K); picosecond (ps) or femtosecond reduces HAZ further but increases cost. 30 ns balances efficacy and safety. Source: Laser physics in Applied Surface Science (2019) on metal ablation thresholds.

repetition_rate.description: Common for pulsed cleaning of reflective metals like iron (reflectivity ~40-60% at 1064 nm); 30 kHz recommended for efficient pulse overlap without excessive heat accumulation, which could lead to oxidation. Higher rates speed up process but risk thermal buildup. Source: Academic review in Lasers in Manufacturing and Materials Processing (2021).

energy_density.description: Fluence range for safe removal of contaminants (e.g., rust, paint) on iron without ablating substrate; damage threshold ~2-4 J/cm² for iron at 1064 nm. 1.5 J/cm² recommended for effective cleaning below threshold, considering iron's absorption. Source: Ablation studies in Optics Express (2017) and Surface & Coatings Technology (2022).

spot_size.description: Beam diameter range for industrial applications; smaller (100-200 μm) for precise surface prep on iron, larger for broad area cleaning. 200 μm recommended for uniform energy distribution on iron surfaces, avoiding hotspots. Source: Equipment specs from Raycus and Coherent lasers, per Journal of Laser Applications (2019).

pass_count.description: Single pass for light contamination (e.g., oils), multi-pass (2-3) for heavy rust on iron to achieve complete removal without increasing fluence. 2 passes recommended for industrial prep, allowing progressive cleaning. Source: Case studies in Industrial Laser Solutions (2020) and practical guidelines from laser cleaning consortia.

overlap_ratio.description: Ensures uniform coverage during scanning on iron; 70% recommended for even fluence distribution and to prevent gaps in cleaning, especially for irregular surfaces. Lower for faster processes, higher for thoroughness. Source: Process optimization in Applied Optics (2018) and manufacturer guidelines from Adapt Laser Systems.
